title: 33rd International Kangaroo Mathematics Contest 2017 — Écolier (Grades 3-4)
disclaimer: Official Math Kangaroo past paper. For personal educational practice only.

questions.question_text: Which of the pieces A–E will fit between the two puzzle pieces so that both equalities are correct? The left piece shows '8−3' and the right piece shows '=2'. (See PDF for figure.)
questions.solution: The left piece 8−3=5, so the middle piece's left side must equal 5. The right piece shows =2, so the middle piece's right expression must equal 2. Only option E gives '=5' on the left and '1+1=2' on the right.
solution_steps: Left equality: 8−3 = 5, so the middle piece's left value must be 5.
Right equality: the middle piece's right expression must equal 2.
Check option E: left side =5 ✓, right side 1+1=2 ✓.

questions.question_text: John looks through the window. He sees half of the kangaroos in the park (see picture). How many kangaroos are there in the park?
questions.solution: The picture shows 6 kangaroos. Since John sees half of all the kangaroos, the total is 6 × 2 = 12.
solution_steps: Count the kangaroos visible through the window: 6.
John sees half, so total = 6 × 2 = 12.

questions.question_text: Two gridded transparent sheets are darkened in some squares. They are both slid on top of a board showing 9 pictures in a 3×3 grid. Only one picture is not covered by either sheet. Which one is it? (See PDF for figure.)
questions.solution: The union of the two sheets' darkened squares covers all cells except the one containing the butterfly. Only the butterfly remains visible.
solution_steps: Mark every cell covered by sheet 1.
Mark every cell covered by sheet 2.
Find the cell not covered by either sheet — it contains the butterfly.

questions.question_text: A picture of footprints was turned upside down. Comparing the original and the upside-down version, which footprints are missing from the upside-down picture? (See PDF for figure.)
questions.solution: After rotating the footprint picture 180°, compare it with the original. The footprints shown in option C are present in the original but missing from the rotated version.
solution_steps: Mentally rotate the original footprint picture 180°.
Compare the rotated image with the picture shown on the right.
Identify which footprint group from the options is absent in the rotated version — it is option C.

questions.question_text: What number is hidden behind the panda? The chain of operations is: 10 + 6 = □, +8 = □, −6 = □, +8 = □, −10 = panda.

In the table correct sums are shown. The column headers are 11, 7, 2. Row 1 header is 6 giving 17, 13, 8. Row 2 header is hidden; its cell under column 2 is covered by '?', and its cell under column 3 (value 2) gives 11. What number is in the box with the question mark? (See PDF for figure.)
questions.solution: The hidden row header r satisfies r + 2 = 11, so r = 9. The question mark cell is r + 7 = 9 + 7 = 16.
solution_steps: From column '2': r + 2 = 11 → r = 9.
Question mark cell is in column '7': r + 7 = 9 + 7 = 16.

questions.question_text: Dolly accidentally broke a mirror into pieces. How many pieces have exactly four sides? (See PDF for figure.)
questions.solution: Examining the broken mirror figure, count the pieces that are quadrilaterals (exactly 4 sides). There are 4 such pieces.
solution_steps: Identify all pieces of the broken mirror (PDF page 3).
For each piece, count its sides.
Pieces with exactly 4 sides: 4 pieces.

questions.question_text: In the figure on the right we see a necklace of six beads. Which one of the options A–E is the same necklace? (See PDF for figure.)
questions.solution: A necklace can be rotated but not flipped. Comparing the bead pattern of the original necklace with each option (allowing rotation), option A matches the original.
solution_steps: Note the pattern of dark and light beads on the original necklace (PDF page 3).
Try rotating the original pattern to match each option.
Option A is identical to the original necklace after rotation.

questions.question_text: The picture shows the front of Ann's house (chimney, 2 windows, 1 door). The back of her house has three windows and no door. What view does Ann see when she looks at the back of her house? (See PDF for figure.)
questions.solution: The back view is the horizontal mirror of the front, with the door replaced by a window. Looking from inside toward the back wall, left and right swap. Option E shows 3 windows and no door in the correct mirrored arrangement.
solution_steps: Mirror the front facade horizontally (left↔right swap).
Replace the door with a window (back has no door).
The resulting view matches option E.

questions.question_text: The equation shows: 4 blue circles + 1 red square = 3 red squares. Which of the following statements is true? (See PDF for figure.)

options.B: 1 blue circle + 1 blue circle + 1 blue circle = 1 red square
options.C: 1 red square + 1 red square + 1 red square = 1 blue circle
options.D: 1 red square + 1 red square = 1 blue circle
options.E: 1 blue circle + 1 blue circle = 1 red square

questions.solution: From 4B + R = 3R: subtract R from both sides → 4B = 2R → 2B = R. So two blue circles equal one red square.
solution_steps: Equation: 4B + R = 3R.
Subtract R: 4B = 2R.
Divide by 2: 2B = R, i.e., blue + blue = red square.

questions.question_text: Balloons are sold in packets of 5, 10, and 25. Marius buys exactly 70 balloons. What is the smallest number of packets he could buy?
questions.solution: 70 = 25 + 25 + 10 + 10 = 2 packets of 25 + 2 packets of 10 = 4 packets total. No combination of 3 packets can reach exactly 70.
solution_steps: Try 3 packets: max is 25+25+25=75, min relevant is 25+25+10=60. Check: 25+25+20? No 20 packet. Can't reach 70 with 3.
Minimum packets = 4.

questions.question_text: Bob folded a piece of paper and cut exactly one hole with a hole puncher. When he unfolded it, he saw 4 holes arranged in a 2×2 pattern. How had Bob folded his piece of paper? (See PDF for figure.)
questions.solution: To get 4 holes arranged in a 2×2 rectangle from a single punch, the paper must be folded in half both horizontally and vertically (into quarters). Option C shows the correct fold lines creating four equal quadrants.
solution_steps: One punch through a folded sheet produces one hole per layer.
4 holes in a 2×2 pattern requires 4 layers → fold twice (in half horizontally and vertically).
Option C shows the fold pattern that creates 4 equal quadrants.

questions.question_text: There is a tournament at the pool. First 13 children signed up and then another 19 signed up. Six teams with an equal number of members are needed. At least how many more children need to sign up so that the six teams can be formed?
questions.solution: Currently 13 + 19 = 32 children. The total must be divisible by 6. 36 ÷ 6 = 6, so 36 − 32 = 4 more children are needed.
solution_steps: Total so far: 13 + 19 = 32.
Next multiple of 6 after 32: 36.
Additional children needed: 36 − 32 = 4.

questions.question_text: Numbers are placed in the cells of a 4×4 square: row 1: 1,2,1,3; row 2: 4,1,1,2; row 3: 1,7,3,2; row 4: 2,1,3,1. Mary finds the 2×2 square where the sum of the four cells is the largest. What is that sum?
questions.solution: Check all nine 2×2 sub-squares. The sub-square at rows 3–4, columns 2–3 gives 7+3+1+3=14, which is the maximum.
solution_steps: Check all 2×2 sub-squares systematically.
Verify no other 2×2 sub-square exceeds 14 — the next highest is 13.
Maximum sum = 14.

questions.question_text: Five routes A–E are drawn between two points on a stepped grid. Which of the routes is the longest? (See PDF for figure.)
questions.solution: Route A (red) traces the outer perimeter of the stepped region, covering the greatest total distance. The other routes take shorter, more direct paths through the interior.
solution_steps: Trace each route on the grid (PDF page 5) and count the total grid units traveled.
Route A follows the outermost boundary, giving the maximum path length.
Route A is the longest.

questions.question_text: Six circles are arranged in a ring. Between them are operations: ×4, −15, +4, ×0, +6. Which number should be written in the circle containing the question mark? (See PDF for figure.)
questions.solution: Label the circles A→B→C→D(?)→E→F→A with operations ×4, −15, +4, ×0, +6 between consecutive circles. Since ×0 produces E=0, then F=0+6=6, A=6×4=24, B=24−15=9, C=9+4=13=D.
solution_steps: The ×0 operation means the circle after it = 0 (call it E=0).
Verify: 13 × 0 = 0 = E ✓.

questions.question_text: The picture shows a group of building blocks and a plan (top-view grid) of the same group. Some ink has dropped onto the plan, covering some numbers. What is the sum of the numbers under the ink blots? (See PDF for figure.)
questions.solution: The visible numbers in the plan are 3, 4, 1, 1, 1, 3, 1. Reading the 3D structure to determine the hidden stack heights, the two ink-blotted cells contain values that sum to 5.
solution_steps: Read the visible cell values from the plan (PDF page 6): 3, 4, 1, 1, 1, 3, 1.
Use the 3D block figure to determine the heights of the two ink-covered cells.
The hidden values sum to 5.

questions.question_text: Two pictures show a train and a bridge (arch). In picture 1, the full scene (train + bridge) spans 340 m. In picture 2, the train is on the bridge and the portion of the bridge extending beyond the train measures 110 m. How long is the train? (See PDF for figure.)
questions.solution: Let T = train length, B = bridge length. From picture 1: T + B = 340. From picture 2: B − T = 110. Adding: 2B = 450, B = 225. Subtracting: 2T = 230, T = 115 m.
solution_steps: Picture 1: T + B = 340.
Picture 2: B − T = 110 (bridge beyond the train).
Add both equations: 2B = 450 → B = 225.
Subtract: 2T = 230 → T = 115 m.

questions.question_text: George starts training at 5:00 PM. The journey from his house to the bus stop takes 5 minutes. The bus journey takes 15 minutes. It takes 5 minutes to go from the bus stop to the field. The bus runs every 10 minutes from 6 AM. At what time at the latest does he have to leave his house to arrive at the field exactly on time? (See PDF for clocks.)
questions.solution: Total travel: 5 + 15 + 5 = 25 min. Must arrive by 5:00 PM, so must board bus by 4:40 PM (15-min ride + 5-min walk = arrive 5:00). Must reach bus stop by 4:40 → leave house by 4:35.
solution_steps: Must arrive at field by 5:00 PM.
Walk from bus stop to field: 5 min → must arrive at bus stop by 4:55 PM.
Bus ride: 15 min → must board bus by 4:40 PM.
Bus runs every 10 min; last bus to catch is the 4:40 PM bus.
Walk from house to bus stop: 5 min → must leave house by 4:35 PM.

questions.question_text: David wants to cook 5 dishes on a stove with only 2 burners. The cooking times are 40 min, 15 min, 35 min, 10 min, and 45 min. He may only remove a dish when it is fully cooked. What is the shortest time in which he can cook all 5 dishes?
questions.solution: Optimal schedule: Burner 1 cooks 40 min then 35 min = 75 min. Burner 2 cooks 45 min, then 15 min (done at 60), then 10 min (done at 70). Both burners finish by 75 min.
solution_steps: Burner 1: 40 min dish (done at 40), then 35 min dish (done at 75).
Burner 2: 45 min dish (done at 45), then 15 min dish (done at 60), then 10 min dish (done at 70).
All dishes done by t = 75 min.
No arrangement finishes all 5 in under 75 min.

questions.question_text: Four brothers have eaten 11 cookies in total. Each of them has eaten at least one cookie and no two of them have eaten the same number. Three of them have eaten 9 cookies in total, and one of them has eaten exactly 3 cookies. How many cookies have been eaten by the boy who ate the largest number?
questions.solution: Three brothers ate 9 total, one of them ate 3, so the other two ate 6 total with all different values ≠ 3: only 1+5=6 works. The fourth brother ate 11−9=2. The four values are {1,2,3,5}, all different. Largest = 5.
solution_steps: Three brothers ate 9 total; one ate 3, so the other two ate 6 total.
Pairs summing to 6, different from each other and from 3: only {1, 5}.
Fourth brother: 11 − 9 = 2.
Four values: {1, 2, 3, 5} — all different ✓.
Largest = 5.

questions.question_text: Zosia has hidden smileys in some cells of a 4×4 table. In other cells she writes the number of smileys in the neighbouring cells (sharing a side or corner). The table shows some numbers: (1,2)=3, (1,3)=3, (2,1)=2, (3,3)=2, (4,2)=1. How many smileys has she hidden? (See PDF for figure.)
questions.solution: Solving the neighbor-count constraints (like Minesweeper), the unique valid placement of smileys that satisfies all given counts results in exactly 5 hidden smileys.
solution_steps: Treat the given numbers as Minesweeper clues (8-neighbor count).
Use constraint propagation: (4,2)=1 limits smileys in its neighborhood.
Systematically place smileys to satisfy all constraints simultaneously.
The unique valid solution has 5 smileys.

questions.question_text: Ten bags each contain a different number of candies from 1 to 10. Five boys each took two bags. Alex got 5 candies, Bob got 7 candies, Charles got 9 candies, and Dennis got 15 candies. How many candies did Eric get?
questions.solution: Total candies = 1+2+…+10 = 55. Alex+Bob+Charles+Dennis = 5+7+9+15 = 36. Eric = 55−36 = 19.
solution_steps: Total of bags 1–10: 55.
Sum of four boys: 5+7+9+15 = 36.
Eric's total: 55 − 36 = 19.

questions.question_text: Kate has 4 flowers with 6, 7, 8, and 11 petals. She tears off one petal from exactly three flowers each round. She stops when she can no longer tear one petal from three flowers (i.e., fewer than 3 flowers still have petals). What is the smallest number of petals that can remain?
questions.solution: Total petals = 6+7+8+11 = 32. Each round removes 3 petals. Since 32 = 3×10 + 2, the minimum possible remaining is 2, and a valid schedule exists that achieves exactly 2 petals remaining when the game stops.
solution_steps: Total petals: 6+7+8+11 = 32.
Each round removes exactly 3 petals.
32 mod 3 = 2, so the theoretical minimum remaining is 2.
A valid schedule can deplete two flowers completely, leaving 2 petals on the remaining flowers when the game stops.
